sections.header: Verses 1-10 — "I Myself Will Fight Against You"

heb.paragraph: God declares: "I myself will fight against you with an outstretched hand (yād nĕṭûyâ) and a mighty arm" (v.5). The phrase "outstretched hand and mighty arm" normally describes God's deliverance of Israel from Egypt (Deut 4:34, 5:15). Its reversal here is devastating: the same divine power that saved Israel from Pharaoh will now be turned against Jerusalem. God fights for the enemy.

panels.ctx: Chapter 21 opens the second major section of Jeremiah (chs. 21-25), sometimes called the "royal oracles." King Zedekiah sends a delegation to Jeremiah during Nebuchadnezzar's siege (588 BC), hoping for a miracle like the deliverance from Sennacherib in Hezekiah's day (2 Kgs 19). God's answer demolishes that hope: there will be no miraculous deliverance. Instead, God himself will fight against Jerusalem. The only path to survival is surrender — "whoever goes out and surrenders to the Babylonians will live" (v.9).

cross.note: The angel's destruction of Sennacherib's army — the precedent Zedekiah hoped to repeat. But this time God fights for Babylon, not against it.
"Outstretched hand and mighty arm" as exodus vocabulary — now reversed against the covenant people.

notes.note: Zedekiah sends Pashhur (a different Pashhur from ch. 20) and the priest Zephaniah to ask Jeremiah to intercede. The request assumes God will "perform wonders" as in the past. The answer will shatter that assumption.
God will "turn against you the weapons in your hands" and fight against Jerusalem himself. The exodus vocabulary reversed: the same power that defeated Pharaoh now targets Judah.

The "way of life and the way of death" (echoing Deut 30:15-19) is redefined: life means surrendering to Babylon; death means staying in the city. Patriotism becomes suicide; apparent treason becomes survival.
Calvin notes the horror of the reversal: "The same God who once stretched out his arm to save now stretches it out to destroy. There is no greater terror than when the Savior becomes the adversary."
The name "Nebuchadnezzar" appears 91 times in Jeremiah. The Hebrew form (nĕbûkadrēʾṣṣar) differs slightly from the Babylonian (Nabû-kudurri-uṣur, "O Nabu, protect the heir").
The phrase "his life will be his reward" (wĕhāyĕtâ-llô napšô lĕšālāl) literally means "his life will be his plunder" — the only spoil of war the survivor carries is his own life.
Lundbom reads this as the opening of the "kings collection" (chs. 21-23). The Zedekiah inquiry frames the royal oracles: the current king's failure introduces the review of all recent kings.

Brueggemann identifies the "way of life/way of death" choice as the most politically subversive statement in the book. Jeremiah tells the king that national survival requires surrendering to the enemy. This is not merely unpopular but treasonous — and it is God's word.

sections.header: Verses 11-14 — Word to the House of David

heb.paragraph: The demand to the royal house: "Administer justice (mišpāṭ) every morning; rescue from the hand of the oppressor the one who has been robbed" (v.12). The daily administration of justice was the king's primary duty in ancient Israel. When mišpāṭ fails, the entire covenant structure collapses.

panels.ctx: The oracle to the "house of David" (v.11) addresses the royal institution rather than an individual king. The demand for justice connects Jeremiah to the broader prophetic tradition (Isa 1:17, Amos 5:24, Mic 6:8). The chapter closes with God announcing judgment on Jerusalem: "I will punish you as your deeds deserve" (v.14). The "forest" that will burn likely refers to the cedar-paneled palace complex.

cross.note: "Let justice roll on like a river" — the prophetic demand for systemic justice that Jeremiah continues.
David "did what was just and right for all his people" — the standard from which the later kings have fallen.

notes.note: The address to the "house of David" — the dynasty rather than the individual. The requirement: administer justice "every morning." Justice is not an occasional act but a daily royal obligation.

God is against "you who live above this valley" — Jerusalem's geographic position on a ridge. The "forest" that will be set on fire likely refers to the palace of the Forest of Lebanon (1 Kgs 7:2), paneled with cedar.
Calvin insists that the demand for daily justice applies to all rulers in every age: "Power without justice is tyranny; monarchy without equity is oppression."
"You who live above this valley, on the rocky plateau" describes Jerusalem's topography — elevated above the surrounding valleys. The city's natural defenses give a false sense of invulnerability.

Lundbom reads this as a transitional oracle linking the Zedekiah inquiry to the individual royal oracles of chapter 22. The demand for justice establishes the criterion by which each king will be measured.

Brueggemann reads "administer justice every morning" as a rejection of royal self-interest. The king exists to serve the people's rights, not the other way around. When this priority inverts, the monarchy loses its legitimacy.

themes.note: Judgment at 10 and Sovereignty at 9 — God fights against his own city. Justice at 8 reflects the demand for royal justice that grounds the entire royal oracle collection.

rows.label: vv.8-10
rows.text: Way of Life / Way of Death

rows.label: vv.11-14
rows.text: Word to the House of David

lit.note: Chapter 21 opens the royal oracle collection with the reversal of exodus theology: God's outstretched arm now fights against his own people. The choice between life and death means choosing between surrender and destruction.

hebtext.note: God declares: "I myself will fight against you with an outstretched hand (yād nĕṭûyâ) and a mighty arm" (v.5). The phrase "outstretched hand and mighty arm" normally describes God's deliverance of Israel from Egypt (Deut 4:34, 5:15). Its reversal here is devastating: the same divine power that saved Israel from Pharaoh will now be turned against Jerusalem. God fights for the enemy.

The demand to the royal house: "Administer justice (mišpāṭ) every morning; rescue from the hand of the oppressor the one who has been robbed" (v.12). The daily administration of justice was the king's primary duty in ancient Israel. When mišpāṭ fails, the entire covenant structure collapses.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Zedekiah's Inquiry During the Siege

translations.text: After that, declares the Lord, I will give Zedekiah king of Judah, his officials and the people in this city who survive the plague, sword and famine, into the hands of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon and to their enemies who want to kill them. He will put them to the sword; he will show them no mercy or pity or compassion.’

Afterward, declares the LORD, I will give Zedekiah king of Judah and his servants and the people in this city who survive the pestilence, sword, and famine into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon and into the hand of their enemies, into the hand of those who seek their lives. He shall strike them down with the edge of the sword. He shall not pity them or spare them or have compassion.’

‘This is what the Lord, the God of Israel, says: I am about to turn against you the weapons of war that are in your hands, which you are using to fight the king of Babylon and the Babylonians who are outside the wall besieging you. And I will gather them inside this city.

‘Thus says the LORD, the God of Israel: Behold, I will turn back the weapons of war that are in your hands and with which you are fighting against the king of Babylon and against the Chaldeans who are besieging you outside the walls. And I will bring them together into the midst of this city.
